source: curated: connection leak after deploy (red herrings: cache warnings, unrelated cron failure)
logs: 12:01:03 INFO deploy: released orders-api v3.2.0 to production (12 pods)
12:02:10 WARN edge-cache: hit ratio 78% (baseline 85%), within tolerance
12:04:11 WARN orders-api: db connection pool usage 85% (17/20)
12:04:40 ERROR cron: nightly-report job failed: S3 bucket 'reports-tmp' not found (failing since last week)
12:05:40 WARN orders-api: db connection pool usage 100% (20/20)
12:05:41 ERROR orders-api: timeout acquiring connection from pool after 5000ms
12:05:52 ERROR orders-api: timeout acquiring connection from pool after 5000ms
12:06:00 WARN api-gateway: /orders p99 latency 8200ms (SLO 500ms)
12:06:22 INFO users-db: CPU 34%, no slow queries — database itself healthy
12:06:30 ERROR orders-api: timeout acquiring connection from pool after 5000ms
12:07:15 INFO orders-api: pool audit: 18 connections held by ReportScheduler.exportDaily, opened 12:02-12:05, never released
12:08:02 WARN orders-api: db connection pool usage 100% (20/20)
12:09:44 WARN edge-cache: hit ratio 74%
12:12:20 INFO oncall: initiated rollback of orders-api
12:15:02 INFO deploy: rolled back orders-api to v3.1.9
12:16:11 INFO orders-api: 18 leaked connections closed on pod shutdown
12:17:45 INFO orders-api: db connection pool usage 20% (4/20)
12:18:30 INFO api-gateway: /orders p99 latency 310ms
reference_root_cause: The orders-api v3.2.0 release introduced a connection leak in ReportScheduler.exportDaily, which opened database connections without releasing them and exhausted the 20-connection pool.
reference_summary: Minutes after the v3.2.0 deploy of orders-api, the ReportScheduler.exportDaily job leaked database connections until the pool was exhausted. Requests then timed out waiting for connections and /orders p99 latency breached the SLO, while the database itself stayed healthy. Rolling back to v3.1.9 released the leaked connections and restored normal latency. The failing nightly-report cron and the cache hit-ratio dip were unrelated.

source: curated: expired client TLS cert (red herrings: deploy earlier that day, GC warning)
logs: 22:41:09 INFO deploy: payments-api v5.7.1 released (config-only change, feature flag cleanup)
23:30:00 INFO payments-api: processed 138 charges in last minute
02:12:44 WARN payments-api: GC pause 610ms on pod payments-api-4
02:59:58 INFO payments-api: processed 141 charges in last minute
03:00:01 ERROR payments-api: TLS handshake with bank-gateway.example.com failed: certificate has expired
03:00:05 ERROR payments-api: TLS handshake with bank-gateway.example.com failed: certificate has expired
03:00:20 ERROR payments-api: charge attempt failed: upstream connection error
03:01:00 WARN api-gateway: /charge error rate 100% (0/57 succeeded)
03:02:14 ERROR payments-api: TLS handshake with bank-gateway.example.com failed: certificate has expired
03:05:33 INFO oncall: paged for payments availability
03:11:02 INFO oncall: rollback of v5.7.1 attempted — errors persist, deploy ruled out
03:19:47 INFO ops: client certificate for bank-gateway.example.com shows notAfter=2026-07-13T03:00:00Z
03:45:10 INFO ops: renewed client certificate for bank-gateway.example.com
03:46:02 INFO payments-api: TLS handshake with bank-gateway.example.com succeeded
03:46:30 INFO payments-api: processed 133 charges in last minute
03:50:00 INFO api-gateway: /charge error rate 0.1%
reference_root_cause: The client TLS certificate used to authenticate to bank-gateway.example.com expired at 03:00 UTC, causing every payment handshake to fail; the earlier v5.7.1 deploy was unrelated.
reference_summary: At exactly 03:00 the client certificate for the bank gateway reached its notAfter time and expired, so all TLS handshakes from payments-api failed and the charge error rate went to 100%. A rollback of the earlier config-only deploy was attempted and ruled out. Payments were down about 46 minutes until the on-call engineer renewed the certificate, after which processing returned to normal.

source: curated: retention misconfig fills broker disk (red herrings: consumer lag alert, kernel warning)
logs: 08:15:00 INFO config: kafka topic 'clickstream' retention.ms changed from 604800000 (7d) to 2592000000 (30d) by data-team
Day2 04:12:51 WARN kafka-broker-3: disk /var/kafka 71% full (was 48% yesterday)
Day3 03:58:10 WARN kafka-broker-3: disk /var/kafka 84% full
Day3 09:20:33 WARN consumers: group 'sessionizer' lag 240k messages (backfill job running, expected)
Day4 02:11:05 WARN kafka-broker-3: disk /var/kafka 93% full
Day4 06:40:12 WARN kernel worker-9: perf event ring buffer overflow (unrelated tracing session)
Day4 11:02:19 ERROR kafka-broker-3: failed to append records to clickstream-7: No space left on device
Day4 11:02:20 ERROR kafka-broker-3: shutting down log dir /var/kafka due to IO error
Day4 11:02:31 WARN cluster: partitions clickstream-7,clickstream-12 offline (leader broker-3 unavailable)
Day4 11:03:02 ERROR ingest-svc: producer send failed: NOT_ENOUGH_REPLICAS, retrying
Day4 11:03:44 ERROR ingest-svc: producer send failed: NOT_ENOUGH_REPLICAS, retrying
Day4 11:05:10 WARN analytics-api: freshness SLA breached: newest event 9m old
Day4 11:21:38 INFO oncall: broker-3 disk 100% — largest dirs are clickstream-* segments spanning 22 days
Day4 11:29:03 INFO ops: retention.ms for 'clickstream' reverted to 604800000 (7d), old segments deleting
Day4 11:58:40 INFO kafka-broker-3: disk /var/kafka 54% full, log dir restored
Day4 12:01:15 INFO cluster: all partitions back online, producer errors cleared
reference_root_cause: A retention configuration change on the clickstream topic (7 days to 30 days) quadrupled stored segment data until broker-3's disk filled, taking its log dir and partitions offline.
reference_summary: Three days after the clickstream topic's retention was raised from 7 to 30 days, accumulated log segments filled broker-3's disk. The broker shut down its log directory on the ENOSPC error, partitions went offline, producers failed with NOT_ENOUGH_REPLICAS, and analytics freshness breached its SLA. Reverting the retention to 7 days and deleting old segments restored the broker and cleared the errors. The consumer-lag and kernel warnings were unrelated noise.

source: curated: debug logging flag enabled in prod (red herrings: autoscaler, network blip)
logs: 14:02:11 INFO flags: 'verbose_request_tracing' enabled for 100% of traffic in prod by user rlee (intended: staging)
14:03:05 INFO search-api: log volume 42x baseline (2.1GB/min)
14:03:40 WARN search-api: CPU 91% across all pods (baseline 35%)
14:04:02 WARN net: transient packet loss 0.4% on rack R12 for 20s, self-recovered
14:04:18 WARN search-api: p99 latency 2900ms (SLO 400ms)
14:04:30 INFO hpa: search-api scaled 10 -> 16 replicas
14:05:12 WARN log-pipeline: ingestion buffer 88% full, applying backpressure
14:05:40 WARN search-api: CPU 94% despite scale-out
14:06:03 WARN search-api: p99 latency 3400ms
14:06:50 INFO hpa: search-api scaled 16 -> 24 replicas (max)
14:07:22 ERROR log-pipeline: dropping events, buffer full
14:09:01 INFO oncall: profiling shows 61% CPU in trace serialization / log formatting
14:11:34 INFO flags: 'verbose_request_tracing' disabled in prod by rlee
14:12:20 INFO search-api: CPU 38%, log volume back to baseline
14:13:05 INFO search-api: p99 latency 350ms
14:15:00 INFO hpa: search-api scaled back to 11 replicas
reference_root_cause: The 'verbose_request_tracing' feature flag was mistakenly enabled for 100% of production traffic instead of staging, and the resulting trace serialization and log formatting consumed most CPU, degrading latency.
reference_summary: A feature flag intended for staging enabled verbose request tracing on all production search-api traffic. Log volume jumped 42x and trace serialization consumed ~60% of CPU, driving p99 latency far past the SLO; autoscaling to max replicas didn't help because every pod paid the logging overhead, and the log pipeline started dropping events. Disabling the flag returned CPU, latency, and log volume to baseline within two minutes. The brief packet loss on rack R12 was unrelated.

source: curated: NTP stopped, clock skew breaks token validation (red herrings: deploy, WAF rule)
logs: 09:12:40 INFO deploy: auth-svc v2.4.4 released (dependency bumps only)
10:05:33 INFO waf: rule 921110 moved from block to monitor mode by security team
11:47:19 WARN node worker-5: chronyd exited (segfault), clock discipline stopped
13:22:08 WARN auth-svc (pod auth-5 on worker-5): token issued with iat 13:25:41 — issuer clock ahead?
13:24:51 ERROR api-gateway: 401 token validation failed: token used before issued (nbf in future)
13:25:02 ERROR api-gateway: 401 token validation failed: token used before issued (nbf in future)
13:26:33 WARN support: login failures reported by ~5% of users, retry sometimes works
13:29:10 ERROR api-gateway: 401 token validation failed: token used before issued (nbf in future)
13:31:47 INFO oncall: failures correlate with tokens minted by pod auth-5 only; other pods clean
13:33:20 INFO oncall: rollback of auth-svc v2.4.4 considered — but pods on other nodes run same version without errors, deploy ruled out
13:36:55 INFO ops: worker-5 system clock is 3m42s ahead of NTP reference; chronyd not running since 11:47
13:39:02 INFO ops: chronyd restarted on worker-5, clock stepped back to reference
13:40:11 INFO auth-svc: tokens from auth-5 validating cleanly
13:42:00 INFO api-gateway: 401 rate back to baseline 0.05%
13:55:12 INFO ops: added alert on node clock offset > 500ms
reference_root_cause: chronyd crashed on worker-5 and the node's clock drifted almost 4 minutes ahead, so tokens minted by the auth pod on that node had future timestamps and failed nbf/iat validation at the gateway.
reference_summary: After the NTP daemon segfaulted on worker-5, the node's clock drifted about 3m42s ahead. The auth-svc pod on that node began issuing tokens with future iat/nbf claims, which the api-gateway rejected as 'used before issued', causing intermittent 401s for roughly 5% of users — only requests whose tokens came from the skewed pod. Restarting chronyd and stepping the clock fixed validation immediately; the v2.4.4 deploy and the WAF rule change were unrelated.
